title: The new non-alignment: How the Middle East is carving out room to maneuver in AI
gist: Atlantic Council fellow Bilal Baloch argues the Gulf states are exploiting a jurisdictional chokepoint exposed by June 2026 US export controls on Anthropic to build a 'non-aligned' AI strategy — deploying over $23 billion in sovereign capital, closing a 0.6%-of-training-data Arabic language gap, and dividing labor across the UAE (infrastructure), Saudi Arabia (industrial data), and Qatar (governance) — even though frontier AI capability still rests with the US and China.

insights.claim: The US government imposed export controls on Anthropic in June 2026, forcing the American AI company to disable its two most capable models globally over concerns about access by foreign nationals, showing that a single government's jurisdiction over an AI firm can pull the technology from users worldwide.
insights.speaker: Bilal Baloch
insights.so_what: Demonstrates that AI availability anywhere depends on the political judgment of the one government with jurisdiction over the model's parent company — a single point of failure for any AI-dependent economy or business.
insights.quote: the US government imposed export controls on Anthropic, an American company, leading it to disable its two most capable models globally, citing the risk of access by foreign nationals

insights.claim: Applying Acemoglu and Robinson's political-economy theory of chokepoints, Baloch argues frontier AI's chokepoint is jurisdictional: whoever has legal authority over the company that owns a model controls the model itself wherever it runs, and incumbents resist relinquishing that control even where doing so would be efficient.
insights.speaker: Bilal Baloch
insights.so_what: Gives a structural lens for why AI regulation clusters around corporate jurisdiction rather than raw technology access — sovereign clients need jurisdictional diversification, not just compute access.
insights.quote: those who control a system’s chokepoints resist relinquishing that control even where doing so would be efficient, because the chokepoint is what decides who else gets to compete at all
insights.locator: That assumption is wrong in a way the political economy

insights.claim: China requires its frontier AI labs to register models with the state before public release, giving Beijing the same kind of jurisdictional chokepoint over Chinese models that Washington exercised against Anthropic.
insights.speaker: Bilal Baloch
insights.so_what: Confirms the chokepoint dynamic is symmetric across both AI superpowers, reinforcing the case for non-aligned states to build independent capacity rather than bet on either bloc.

insights.claim: Models trained mainly on Silicon Valley data encode a particular cultural and cognitive vantage point rather than neutral logic — for example, a healthcare AI calibrated on American clinical data can misrepresent reality when advising patients in Amman — making model-ecosystem diversification a normative as well as competitive goal.
insights.speaker: Bilal Baloch
insights.so_what: Signals a durable demand driver for regionally trained/localized models in the Gulf and broader Global South, a market opportunity distinct from raw compute competition.
insights.quote: When a healthcare AI calibrated on American clinical data advises patients in Amman, it misrepresents the world to the people it is meant to serve.
insights.locator: There is also a normative dimension

insights.claim: Gulf sovereign wealth funds — the Abu Dhabi Investment Authority, Mubadala, and the Saudi Public Investment Fund — deployed more than $23 billion into AI and digital infrastructure in 2025, with Mubadala's $12.9 billion the single largest sovereign AI commitment globally that year.
insights.speaker: Bilal Baloch
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of Gulf capital now competing for AI infrastructure deals — a funding source increasingly independent of US or Chinese state permission for global AI infrastructure players to track as both partner and competitor.
insights.locator: Gulf sovereign wealth funds, including the Abu Dhabi Investment Authority

insights.claim: Gulf capital independence buys negotiating leverage but does not remove the underlying jurisdictional chokepoint, since the silicon and frontier models that Gulf capital purchases still originate almost entirely from the US and Chinese jurisdictions.
insights.speaker: Bilal Baloch
insights.so_what: Tempers the bullish 'Gulf AI independence' narrative — capital scale alone does not solve the supply-chain dependency on US/China-origin chips and models.
insights.quote: the silicon and the frontier models that capital still has to purchase come from the same two jurisdictions the chokepoint runs through

themes.theme: The Arabic-language gap as market opening

insights.claim: Arabic is natively spoken by more than 400 million people and is the fourth most-used language on the internet, yet makes up only about 0.6% of training data in leading large language models, leaving every Arabic-language-interface sector underserved at scale.
insights.speaker: Bilal Baloch
insights.so_what: Identifies a large, quantifiable underserved-language market — a specific data/product gap regional AI labs and localization vendors can target ahead of US/Chinese incumbents.
insights.locator: The second is the Arabic language gap

insights.claim: Abu Dhabi's Technology Innovation Institute has built Falcon Arabic, trained on native, non-machine-translated Arabic text, directly targeting the language gap that dominant multilingual models leave unaddressed.
insights.speaker: Bilal Baloch
insights.so_what: A concrete, named product to watch as a regional-model bet; its traction would validate the localized-LLM thesis as a defensible niche versus US/Chinese frontier labs.
insights.locator: Abu Dhabi’s Technology Innovation Institute has responded

insights.claim: The Gulf states' simultaneous engagement with American AI companies and Chinese hardware suppliers is not opportunistic hedging but a deliberate assertion of agency, comparable to Nehru's Cold War-era Non-Aligned Movement, which was likewise dismissed by critics as equivocation.
insights.speaker: Bilal Baloch
insights.so_what: Reframes Gulf dual-sourcing of US models and Chinese hardware as durable strategic policy rather than temporary arbitrage, implying it persists regardless of US pressure to pick a side.
insights.locator: The strategic logic here is one Washington

insights.claim: Three Gulf states are converging on three different layers of the AI opportunity by separate calculation: the UAE is building infrastructure for a Global South unwilling to inherit a single superpower's tech stack, Saudi Arabia is embedding AI into Vision 2030 via the Saudi Data and Artificial Intelligence Authority using Aramco's proprietary data, and Qatar is positioning around AI governance and diplomatic convening.
insights.speaker: Bilal Baloch
entities: Saudi Data and Artificial Intelligence Authority
insights.so_what: Maps three distinct, investable Gulf AI plays — infrastructure (UAE), industrial-data AI (Saudi/Aramco), and governance/diplomacy (Qatar) — rather than a single undifferentiated 'Gulf AI' bet.
insights.locator: The United Arab Emirates is building the infrastructure node

insights.claim: The Gulf's AI talent pipeline remains the binding constraint on frontier capability; institutions like MBZUAI in the UAE and KAUST in Saudi Arabia echo the generational bet India made when it built its Institutes of Technology, though building deep research capacity is still a generational project.
insights.speaker: Bilal Baloch
insights.so_what: Flags talent and research depth — not capital or data — as the real long-horizon bottleneck for Gulf AI ambitions, relevant to timing any thesis on regional frontier-model competitiveness.
insights.locator: The talent pipeline remains the binding constraint

insights.claim: None of the Gulf's current moves yet constitute frontier AI capability — labs in Palo Alto and Hangzhou still hold that ground — but the states are building structural agency in capital, data, language models, and governance positioning that could compound into durable advantage, comparable to how patient investment in football infrastructure let Morocco draw with Brazil at the 2026 World Cup.
insights.speaker: Bilal Baloch
insights.so_what: Sets realistic expectations: this is a multi-year positioning story, not an imminent frontier-capability challenge to the US or China — useful for calibrating investment time horizons.
insights.quote: the labs in Palo Alto and Hangzhou hold that ground for now
insights.locator: None of this yet claims frontier capability

top_entities: Saudi Data and Artificial Intelligence Authority
